feed.subtitle: I'm an analyst, sailor, & trader near SF. ghpages+jekyll+minima+chivo/inconsolata

entry.title: Stock Data with Jupyter
entry.content:
This is an initial dive into Jupyter notebooks, the self-contained Python environment. The below post is exported directly from a Jupyter Notebook. The tables below each code block are the API responses.

I am using the BarChart.com OnDemand API to call getHistory() and receive minute-level stock price data for a basket of stocks. Then I do bar analysis to find potential buy spots. This analysis is simply counting instances of minutely bars closing on highs.

To use the code yourself, you will need to setup a python environment, jupyter notebook or otherwise. Then, you’ll need to request a free BarChart.com OnDemand API key, and plug it into the code.

Last, set the file path for your project directory, and in that folder create a folder ‘Stock_Price_Data’ to export data to, and a folder ‘assets’ for your list. In ‘assets’ create an Excel file ‘Watchlist’. Starting in row 4 with the header, have names in the first column and tickers in the second column.

Fetch the data from BarChart

Here we call the barchart getHistory API. This is an adaptation of BlackArb’s Python tutorial.

Steps:

  • import libraries
  • set directories and API key
  • build URL to call API
  • get_minute_data() function
  • call the function

outputs: symbol, timestamp, tradingDay, open, high, low, close, volume, openInterest

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

#----------IMPORT LIBRARIES----------#
import time
t0 = time.clock()

import pandas as pd
from pandas.tseries.offsets import BDay

import numpy as np
import datetime as dt

from copy import copy
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore',category=pd.io.pytables.PerformanceWarning)
 

    
#----------SET DIRECTORIES----------#
    
project_dir = r'C:\Users/user/fakepath/' 
price_path = project_dir + r'Stock_Price_Data\\'

# header=3 to skip metadata   
spy_components = pd.read_excel(project_dir +\
                             'assets/Watchlist.xls', header=3)
syms = spy_components.Identifier.dropna()
syms = syms.drop(syms.index[-1]).sort_values()


apikey = 'insert API key'



#----------BUILD URL----------#

def construct_barChart_url(sym, start_date, freq, api_key=apikey):
    '''Function to construct barchart api url'''
    
    url = 'http://marketdata.websol.barchart.com/getHistory.csv?' +\
            'key={}&symbol={}&type={}&startDate={}'.format(api_key, sym, freq, start_date)
    return url




#----------F(X) DEFINITION----------#

def get_minute_data():
    '''Function to Retrieve <= 3 months of minute data for SP500 components'''
    
    # YYYY MM DD HH MM SS
    start = '20171001000000'
    #end = d
    freq = 'minutes'    
    prices = {}
    symbol_count = len(syms)
    
    try:
        for i, sym in enumerate(syms, start=1):
            api_url = construct_barChart_url(sym, start, freq, api_key=apikey)
            try:
                csvfile = pd.read_csv(api_url, parse_dates=['timestamp'])
                csvfile.set_index('timestamp', inplace=True)
                prices[sym] = csvfile
            except:
                continue

            print('{}..[fetched] | {} of {} tickers |'.format(sym, i, symbol_count)) 
    except Exception as e: 
        print(e)
    finally:
        pass
    
    px = pd.Panel.from_dict(prices)
    px.major_axis = px.major_axis.tz_localize('utc').tz_convert('US/Pacific')
    return px



#----------CALL FUNCTION----------#

pxx = get_minute_data()
pxx



# timer
secs      = np.round( ( time.clock()  - t0 ), 4 )
time_secs = "{timeSecs} seconds to run".format(timeSecs = secs)
mins      = np.round( ( (  time.clock() ) -  t0 )  / 60, 4 ) 
time_mins = "| {timeMins} minutes to run".format(timeMins = mins)
hours     = np.round( (  time.clock()  -  t0 )  / 60 / 60, 4 ) 
time_hrs  = "| {timeHrs} hours to run".format(timeHrs = hours)

print( time_secs, time_mins, time_hrs )

AAPL..[fetched] | 1 of 9 tickers |
AMZN..[fetched] | 2 of 9 tickers |
BIDU..[fetched] | 3 of 9 tickers |
FB..[fetched] | 4 of 9 tickers |
GOOGL..[fetched] | 5 of 9 tickers |
GS..[fetched] | 6 of 9 tickers |
IBB..[fetched] | 7 of 9 tickers |
NFLX..[fetched] | 8 of 9 tickers |
TSLA..[fetched] | 9 of 9 tickers |
('49.2381 seconds to run', '| 0.8206 minutes to run', '| 0.0137 hours to run')

Select stock

Choose a ticker to analyze, and test the output.

stock = 'NFLX'

ohlc = pxx[stock]
ohlc.head(1)
symbol tradingDay open high low close volume
timestamp
2017-10-02 06:30:00-07:00 NFLX 2017-10-02 182.11 182.22 181.815 181.945 74650

1-min accumulation bars

Where 1 min candlestick closes on highs, flag as accumulated.

#create accum column: for closing on highs, assign 1
ohlc["accum"] = np.where(ohlc["high"] == ohlc["close"], 1, 0)

#list matches
accumulation_bars = ohlc[(ohlc.accum == 1)]
accumulation_bars[["high", "close", "accum"]].tail(3)
high close accum
timestamp
2017-11-07 12:38:00-08:00 195.38 195.38 1
2017-11-07 12:56:00-08:00 195.87 195.87 1
2017-11-07 13:00:00-08:00 195.89 195.89 1

Consecutive accumulation

Add the previous accumulated (or not) to current row

Measures when two 1-min candlesticks in a row close at highs.

#create conf column adding prev column
ohlc["conf"] = ohlc.accum + ohlc.accum.shift(1)

#where conf is 2, make a table
confirmation = ohlc[(ohlc.conf == 2)]
confirmation[["high", "close", "conf"]].tail(3)
high close conf
timestamp
2017-11-06 09:44:00-08:00 201.54 201.54 2
2017-11-06 10:09:00-08:00 201.32 201.32 2
2017-11-06 12:36:00-08:00 199.97 199.97 2

Roll up to daily signal

sum the consecutive accumulation by day

Return the 3 biggest daily signals

#combine minutes into days
signal = confirmation.resample('D',how='sum')

#sort based on sum(conf)
#signal.sort_values(['double'], ascending=[False])
signal.nlargest(3,'conf')
accum conf
timestamp
2017-10-03 00:00:00-07:00 22 44
2017-11-02 00:00:00-07:00 10 20
2017-11-03 00:00:00-07:00 10 20
entry.summary: This is an initial dive into Jupyter notebooks, the self-contained Python environment. The below post is exported directly from a Jupyter Notebook. The tables below each code block are the API responses.

entry.title: Traffic and self-driving ethics
entry.content:
I recently moved to San Jose, so I have to commute on the 87 and 101. On the way home, the merge from 101 to 87 is particularly atrocious.

It’s a right side combined on-ramp/off-ramp. The on-ramp Trimble Rd -> 101 becomes the offramp 101 -> 87. So cars are exiting the lane at the same time cars are entering the lane. This combined use of a single lane creates bumper to bumper traffic.

There are two approaches to merging from 101 to 87.

Polite southbound commuters will merge ASAP, to allow traffic continuing on 101 past 87 to flow.

Some jerks zoom forward in the 2nd right lane to skip the 97 lineup.
Usually these people wedge themselves in too late, slowing the whole merge lane to a stop.
Other times they are stuck stopped in the middle of the freeway with their blinker on, as the cars already in the right lane prevent them from merging. (ostensibly to teach them a lesson)

It’s a huge mess, partially due to the road design and partially due to driver behavior. I can’t help but think of a self-driving future.

My questions are:

  • Will self-driving cars drive aggressively to the detriment of the whole?

  • Or will they only do what’s best for the group? -If beta by design, will regular cars be able to drive even more aggressively?

  • Will self-driving aggression be user-controlled?
    • If user-controlled, will there be incentives to keep levels low? Wouldn’t everyone set the aggression at 10/10? (related to insurance premiums)
  • Or decided by the car?
    • If car-controlled, will each car be competing on time or other convenience factors?

Kinda looking forward to their debut, kinda not. I’m afraid the future will be frustrating too, but in a different way, where all our cars are unwilling to just scoot ahead.

Check out this Fortune article about how Google is humanizing their cars.

I have heard the theory that blockchain will enable self-driving cars to have their own ledger, and be incentivized to seek out gas, provide cheaper rates, etc. in pursuit of capital accumulation. In this case, I am sure these cars won’t be “shy.”
entry.summary: I recently moved to San Jose, so I have to commute on the 87 and 101. On the way home, the merge from 101 to 87 is particularly atrocious.

entry.title: Van Damme camping
entry.content:
Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

Jake, Brian, and I camped at Van Damme State Park Friday and Saturday night. (Van Damme, gurl)

It was a 5hr drive from San Jose in rush hour traffic! Luckily we did take the scenic routes closer to the coast, which ended up being further, but without traffic and along 2 lane roads. As Google Maps tends to adjust ETA adding up to half an hour as traffic rises, we definitely saved time thinking through our route.

The site was #37 and was situated nicely on top of a rise, with bathrooms nearby and good sunlight in the morning.

We kept meals to a couple ingredients to save space and reduce costs:

  • Pasta (tortellini, alfredo)
  • Smores (graham crackers, marshmallows, hershey’s)
  • Quesadillas (tortillas, mexican cheese, sausage)
  • Oatmeal (oats, peanut butter)
  • Mandarin Oranges

Our original plan was to take a sea cave kayaking tour, but it was expensive and full. So we called an audible and drove up to Fort Bragg. Here’s the places we went:

  • Glass Beach, to see the glass and swim. Cold water filled with kelp forests! The Van Damme camp ranger told us Glass Beach used to be a literal dump, then the county was told to clean it up. All the broken bottles became glass beach. Now that’s good marketing. We talked to an abalone diver who was tagging some shells, and he explained he was part of a competition taking place today.

  • Caspar Beach, where a Triton Free Diving competition weigh in was going on. We saw abalone, cod, and scallops the size of a baseball mitt.

  • North Coast Brewery, home of Brother Thelonius Belgian Style Abbey Ale (appley) and the Rasputin Russian Imperial Stout (chocolatey).

  • Pygmy Forest Trail. Pygmy means short, and this forest’s stunted growth is caused by poor soil, as the area sat underwater many years ago. There were huckleberrys (very edible!) along the boardwalk through the pygmy forest, followed by a wide fire trail to the next intersection about 2 1/2 miles up, surrounded by redwoods.

  • Big River beach, to enjoy a beer and catch the sunset after the hike. The sun sets over the point instead of the ocean, so you get to enjoy the flash of the sun setting, then a few extra minutes of light to finish your beer as the actual sunset occurs. The area in the back, a broad beach along the big river, looked excellent for hanging out during the day. (noted)

  • Russian River Brewery, home of Pliny the Elder/Younger. A great pit stop on the way back. It was very busy with locals on Sunday as it is happy hour all day, so we ate down the street at a burger joint and came back. I tried the Golden Ale and grabbed a growler of Pliny.
entry.summary: Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

entry.title: Finless Foods fun
entry.content:
I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

Zois -my housemate Ian’s friend from time in Chile- stayed on our couch for a few days. He invited me to Massive Attack at 1015 Folsom with Emily, a friend visiting SF. Emily was staying in SoMa with Mike, who she met in Taiwan.

Mike lives with Brian, and together they run Finless Foods, a new food startup creating fish without fishing or farming. They’re growing fish meat from stem cells. Finless is creating surimi (fish paste) in the lab now.

  • Their fish is healthier than caught fish which contains mercury and plastic.
  • It is also healthier than farmed fish which contains antibiotics and hormones.
  • As the fish is grown without a body of water, production can be geographically distributed.
  • Since no fish are harmed, Finless appeals to ethical vegans.

An interesting question my housemate Ian asked is: can this fish be produced in a way that is less carbon intensive than the usual process?

When Zois and I picked up Mike, Brian, and co-worker Jihyun, they had just wrapped up an initial tasting. “This is three quarters of the company in the backseat, right now!” they joked as they piled in. Mike was looking forward to “only” having to speak in front of 1000 people the following week. Easy, in comparison.

Zois, Emily, Mike, and I went to Massive Attack together. It was a fun show, closing with “Teardrop”. Afterwards, we took a peek at the IndieBio incubator which was right around the corner.

In the morning, Mike mentioned that he had actually felt unstressed for the first time in months. Balance is important! (An hour later he was off to New York on a plane.)

Here’s the link to the “1000 people” talk mentioned above - IndieBio SF Demo Day 5.

Mike comes on just before the 1:03:00 mark.

Good luck to Finless Foods!!
entry.summary: I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

entry.title: Why do blogs die?
entry.content:
Blogs are everywhere. You probably have an old one under your bed somewhere.

They’re started by teachers, travellers, investors, product people and developers.

They’re hosted on Blogger, Wordpress, or Tumblr. Or on Squarespace, Ghost, Wix, Weebly, etc.

They’re painstakenly designed, categorized, and paletted.

Then there’s 5 posts total and the most recent one is from over 2 years ago. It’s tragic really. So much intent and good will, stopped dead in its tracks.

Here’s this person who put in a ton of platform work to connect with the world, and you can just see how quickly it fizzled and died.

Why?

A) People make blogs about stuff they want to be an expert in, not about stuff they’re already experts in. B) Turns out, it’s tiring to try to be something you’re not.

The author is neither writing for themselves nor from actual knowledge. They’re writing to some pie-in-the-sky audience and putting on a persona to attract them.

It gets harder to write over time because there’s no new ideas being discussed, only regurgitation.

This ecosystem of B.S is particularly obvious with LinkedIn published articles lately.

A job seeker mashes his keyboard for a few hours to create a listicle (5 ways to be more productive at work). Upon reading it he thinks to himself in horror, “What did I just write?” Given the huge sunk cost of having written the article, he shrugs and hits SUBMIT, figuring some actual professional will be able to understand what he’s getting at.
Later a reader clicks on and skims through the article, thinking to himself, “What is this that I’m reading?” Confused and wasted, he shrugs and hits LIKE, figuring someone will see his like and think he understood it.

We can all agree the internet doesn’t need any more of that kind of stuff. It’s wasting everyone’s time.

So guess what? This is going to be the exact opposite.

I’m going to write randomly and colloquially, and only about things that I understand and that interest me. There will be no standard length post, and no general topic. Posts will be edited over time.

Buckle up!
entry.summary: Blogs are everywhere. You probably have an old one under your bed somewhere.
